chapter: Chapter: How the State Government Works (Social and Political Life-II)

questions.question: What determines whether a political party can form the government in a state?

options.A: If it has the most experienced leaders.
options.B: If its MLAs have won more than half the number of constituencies in the state.
options.C: If the Governor belongs to that party.
options.D: If it has the most money for campaigning.

questions.explanation: A party needs a simple majority (more than 50% of seats) to be invited to form the government.

questions.question: The Governor of a state is appointed by the Central Government to ensure that:

options.A: The state wins all sports competitions.
options.B: The State Government works within the rules and regulations of the Constitution.
options.C: The Chief Minister follows the Governor's personal orders.
options.D: The state spends all its money every year.

questions.explanation: The Governor acts as a link between the Centre and the State, ensuring constitutional propriety.

questions.question: In the Legislative Assembly, what is the primary role of the 'Opposition'?

options.A: To stop the government from working at all.
options.B: To question government decisions and highlight different issues.
options.C: To help the Chief Minister make laws.
options.D: To manage the police department.

questions.explanation: The opposition holds the government accountable by debating policies and suggesting alternatives.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a 'Coalition Government'?

options.A: A government run by the army.
options.B: A government formed by two or more political parties together when no single party has a majority.
options.C: A government that has no Chief Minister.
options.D: A government where only one person makes all decisions.

questions.explanation: When no single party reaches the magic number (majority), multiple parties join forces to reach that number.

questions.question: What is the 'dual role' played by some members of the Legislative Assembly?

options.A: They are both voters and candidates.
options.B: They are both MLAs and Ministers.
options.C: They are both Governors and Chief Ministers.
options.D: They work for both the State and Central government.

questions.explanation: Some MLAs are selected to be ministers, meaning they represent their constituency and head a government department.

questions.question: The Legislative Assembly is not just a place for making laws, but also a place where:

options.A: The Governor lives.
options.B: Opinions are expressed and action is demanded from the government.
options.C: Trials for criminals are held.
options.D: Taxes are collected directly from people.

questions.explanation: It is a forum for debate where representatives voice the concerns of the people they represent.

questions.question: After the debate in the Assembly, why do ministers hold 'Press Conferences'?

options.A: To chat with their friends.
options.B: To explain the steps the government is taking to address specific issues.
options.C: To tell people to stop asking questions.
options.D: To show off their new clothes.

questions.explanation: Press conferences are a way for the executive to communicate their actions and policies to the general public through the media.

questions.question: Who is the head of the executive branch of the state government?

options.A: The Chief Justice
options.B: The Chief Minister
options.C: The Speaker
options.D: The Collector

questions.explanation: The Chief Minister, along with the council of ministers, forms the executive branch that implements laws.

questions.question: Laws are made on various issues like health and sanitation. Who actually 'implements' these laws?

options.A: The MLAs who are in the opposition.
options.B: The various government departments (The Executive).
options.C: The Governor alone.
options.D: The judges in the High Court.

questions.explanation: While the legislature makes the laws, the executive (government departments) is responsible for putting them into action.

questions.question: In the case of Patalpuram, what immediate action did the Chief Minister take?

options.A: He resigned from his post.
options.B: He visited the families and announced compensation and funds for drinking water.
options.C: He told people to move to another state.
options.D: He closed all hospitals in the area.

questions.explanation: The Chief Minister visited the affected area to assess the situation and provide administrative relief.

questions.question: Why did the MLA from Akhandagaon suggest that the government was not taking enough steps?

options.A: Because there were no schools in his area.
options.B: Because hospitals were in bad shape and there was a shortage of doctors.
options.C: Because he wanted to become the Chief Minister.
options.D: Because it was raining too much.

questions.explanation: The MLA pointed out the lack of medical infrastructure and staff to handle the health crisis.

questions.question: The term 'Government' in common language often refers to:

options.A: Every single person in the country.
options.B: Government departments and the various ministers who head them.
options.C: Only the police officers.
options.D: The building where the Assembly meets.

questions.explanation: This is known as the Executive part of the government, which carries out the daily administration.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the Legislature and the Executive?

options.A: They are the same thing.
options.B: The Executive is part of the Legislature, and the Legislature supervises the Executive's work.
options.C: The Executive tells the Legislature what laws to make.
options.D: They have no connection with each other.

questions.explanation: MLAs (Legislature) approve the work and budget of the Ministers (Executive) and can question them.

questions.question: How do people in a democracy show that they are unhappy with the government's work?

options.A: By organizing rallies and protests.
options.B: By writing in newspapers.
options.C: By asking their MLAs questions.
options.D: All of the above.

questions.explanation: Democracy provides multiple channels like media, direct protests, and through representatives to express dissent.

questions.question: What does a 'Constituency' represent in an election?

options.A: A group of people with the same name.
options.B: A specific geographical area from which a representative is elected.
options.C: A political party's office.
options.D: The list of laws made by the government.

questions.explanation: Every state is divided into areas called constituencies to ensure representation for all regions.

questions.question: During the assembly session, why did some MLAs blame the government for the deaths in Patalpuram?

options.A: Because the government didn't build enough parks.
options.B: Because of the lack of clean drinking water and poor sanitation facilities.
options.C: Because the government was too rich.
options.D: Because the roads were not painted.

questions.explanation: Opposition MLAs argued that the deaths were due to the government's failure to provide basic hygiene and water services.

questions.question: Who has the power to decide which MLA will become a Minister?

options.A: The Governor
options.B: The Chief Minister
options.C: The Prime Minister
options.D: The People

questions.explanation: The Chief Minister selects the members for their cabinet, although the Governor formally appoints them.

questions.question: The decisions made in the Legislative Assembly must be ______ by the ministers.

options.A: Ignored
options.B: Approved/Implemented
options.C: Kept secret
options.D: Deleted

questions.explanation: The executive is responsible for acting upon the decisions and laws passed in the house.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'Wallpaper Project' mentioned in the chapter?

options.A: To decorate the classroom.
options.B: To research and share information on a particular topic of interest.
options.C: To hide the dirty walls of the school.
options.D: To learn how to draw.

questions.explanation: It is an educational activity described in the book to help students engage with real-world issues like the Patalpuram health crisis.

questions.question: An MLA represents the people of their constituency. This means they should:

options.A: Only listen to the Chief Minister.
options.B: Voice the problems and needs of their constituency in the Assembly.
options.C: Live in the Legislative Assembly building.
options.D: Not answer any questions from the public.

questions.explanation: The primary duty of an MLA is to be the voice of their local voters in the state legislature.

questions.question: In the example of Himachal Pradesh, how many seats did the INC win to get a majority in 2017?
questions.explanation: The BJP (not INC in that specific year/example) won 44 seats, which is more than half of 68, giving them the majority.

questions.question: Why is it important for the ruling party to have a majority?

options.A: So they can have more holidays.
options.B: To ensure they can pass laws and have stability in the government.
options.C: To keep the Governor happy.
options.D: So they don't have to talk to the opposition.

questions.explanation: Without a majority, a government might fall easily or find it impossible to pass important legislation.

questions.question: What is 'Public Works Department' (PWD) responsible for?

options.A: Hospitals
options.B: Roads and buildings
options.C: Farming
options.D: Schools

questions.explanation: The PWD is one of the departments through which the state government carries out infrastructure work.

questions.question: When a reporter asks a minister a question at a press conference, it shows:

options.A: The reporter is being rude.
options.B: Accountability of the government to the people through the media.
options.C: The minister doesn't know anything.
options.D: The conference is over.

questions.explanation: The media acts as a bridge, ensuring the government explains its actions to the public.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a way the government addresses a problem?

options.A: Making new laws.
options.B: Ordering departments to take action.
options.C: Holding press conferences.
options.D: Ignoring the opposition's questions entirely.

questions.explanation: In a democracy, the government is obliged to respond to the opposition and the public; ignoring them is anti-democratic.
